title: Felting with naturally coloured fleece
subtitle: Working with Jacob, Shetland, and Soay fleece without dyes
excerpt: Jacob provides black-and-white patterning; Shetland offers moorit, grey, and fawn tones; Soay gives warm brown and dark grey. This guide covers breed-specific felting behaviour and how to blend naturally coloured wools to extend your tonal range without any dyeing.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from published breed wool guides and guild teaching on natural colour felting. Jacob, Shetland, and Soay breed characteristics are well documented in UK rare-breed fleece literature. The blending approach is standard practice; no single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fleece from Jacob sheep, naturally occurring in black and white. Produces speckled or blended tones without dyeing.
Fleece from Shetland sheep. Available in a wide range of natural colours including white, grey, fawn, moorit (reddish-brown), and black.
A reddish-brown natural wool colour found in Shetland and some other northern breeds. The name comes from the Old Norse for heather.
Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Felting using warm water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibres to interlock and shrink into a cohesive fabric.
The length of a single lock of fleece, measured from the cut end to the tip. Affects how the wool drafts and felts.
A measure of the average diameter of wool fibres in micrometres. Lower numbers indicate finer, softer fibre; higher numbers indicate coarser fibre.

content.text: Most felting tutorials start with dyed merino in bright colours. Naturally coloured fleece from heritage breeds gives you a different palette. The greys, browns, blacks, and fawns of Jacob,
, and Soay cannot be copied by mixing dyes. Each colour is in the fibre itself, not on the surface. The finished felt has a depth that dyed wool cannot replicate.
The breeds and their colour ranges

is the most distinctively patterned naturally coloured wool available in the UK. Individual fleeces contain sections of black, dark grey, and white, sometimes within a single lock. Blended together, these produce a warm grey-brown with a slightly tweedy texture. Used unblended, they give strong contrast. The fleece is medium-grade with a
typically in the range of 28-35 microns. It felts reliably but takes longer than fine merino to develop full density.
comes in more registered natural colours than any other UK breed: white, shaela (grey with a slight blue cast), grey, fawn,
(reddish-brown), dark brown, black, and various blends. The fleece is finer than Jacob, typically 23-27 microns, and felts more readily. The shorter
easily and is forgiving to work with.

Soay. Soay is a primitive breed from the St Kilda archipelago, and its fleece is the most rustic of the three. The natural colours are warm brown and very dark grey (almost black). The fleece contains some kemp (coarse medullated fibre) which does not felt and is visible as a slight texture in the finished surface. Soay fleece is coarser than Shetland (30-35 microns) and requires more water, heat, and agitation to felt fully. It is not a beginner breed for fine-surface work, but it produces a wonderfully textured rustic finish suited to natural objects and landscape pieces.

Pull wisps of each colour in the proportion you want in the finished blend. For a mid-grey from Jacob, use roughly equal parts black and white roving. For a warm fawn from Shetland, combine white and moorit in a 2:1 ratio.
Layer the wisps over each other and pull through your hands several times to blend. You can do this by hand for coarse blending, or through hand cards (if you have them) for a more even mix. For
, a hand blend is usually sufficient. For needle felting surface work, a finer blend gives a more even tone.

Test-felt a small sample before committing to a large piece. Natural colours can shift slightly during felting: black can soften to very dark grey; moorit may warm toward orange-brown when wet. Always test a 10 cm square first.

Jacob and Soay felt more slowly than fine merino because their fibre diameter is larger and their surface scales are less pronounced. Both breeds respond to the same adjustments: hotter water (65°C rather than 60°C), more soap, longer agitation, and a more pronounced temperature shock between hot and cold rinses. The additional time is not a fault; it is the nature of the fibre. Rushing either breed produces a surface that looks felted but pulls apart under tension.
of Shetland means it can pill slightly at the surface if over-agitated in the early pre-felt stage. Work gently at first: press and smooth rather than scrub until the surface is firm.
UK rare-breed farms often sell raw fleece by post at very low cost compared to dyed commercial

. Raw fleece needs washing (scouring) before use: soak in very hot water with a small amount of washing-up liquid for 20 minutes, lift out without agitating, and repeat until the water runs clear. Do not agitate the raw fleece during scouring or it will felt in the basin. A processed naturally coloured
is available from specialist suppliers if you prefer not to scour raw fleece. The
roving from specialist sellers is typically pre-scoured and ready to use.

items.symptom: Jacob fleece not felting evenly across a panel
items.cause: Black and white sections of the same fleece felt at slightly different rates because the fibre diameter can vary between pigmented and unpigmented locks
items.fix: Work the surface for longer, paying extra attention to any areas that are lagging. A consistent hot temperature helps equalise the rate.

items.symptom: Soay surface looks hairy rather than smooth after felting
items.cause: Kemp fibres in the fleece do not full and stand proud of the surface
items.fix: This is a characteristic of the breed rather than a felting error. If a smoother surface is needed, shear the surface lightly with sharp scissors after the piece is dry.

items.symptom: Moorit Shetland colour looks washed-out after fulling
items.cause: The fleece lost some surface bloom during the hot-water process
items.fix: A 5-minute soak in cold water with a tablespoon of white vinegar at the end of the process can restore slight colour depth. Rinse with cool clean water afterward.
